data.title: Spirit Airlines in `very advanced discussions' with US on funding
data.description:
Spirit Airlines (FLYYQ) is in "very advanced discussions" with the US government for a financing package to help the distressed carrier.

Spirit's lawyer Marshall Huebner declined to discuss the terms in New York bankruptcy court on Thursday, according to a Bloomberg account of the court proceeding. 

Bloomberg reported on Wednesday that the discount airline was in talks for $500 million of financing in exchange for warrants that could give the the US government the option to own as much as a 90% stake in Spirit (FLYQQ).

The talks come after President Donald Trump on Tuesday said he would like to see a buyer for the distressed airline and said he may be open to the government helping out the discount carrier.

Shares of Spirit fell 19% on Thursday.

data.title: White House warns: China's AI theft exposed ahead of Trump-Xi summit
data.description:
The White House on Thursday accused China of running “industrial-scale” campaigns to steal AI technology from U.S. companies, signaling a potential escalation in the ongoing tech war. 

The claims reportedly come just weeks before a planned meeting between Donald Trump and Xi Jinping—raising the stakes ahead of the summit.According to a memo shared by Michael Kratsios, Director of the Office of Science and Technology Policy, the U.S. has evidence that foreign entities—mainly based in China—are using “tens of thousands of proxy accounts” and jailbreaking techniques to systematically extract American breakthroughs.

The official memo says that while AI “distillation” is a legitimate technique to build smaller models, these alleged campaigns are misusing it—copying performance at a much lower cost and possibly removing important safety and security layers, as reported by the Financial Times. 

The issue gained traction recently after allegations around China’s DeepSeek using similar methods. Now, the US-China AI war just got intensified.Here’s what the White House memo under the Trump administration plans to do to tackle this threat:

  • Share intelligence with U.S. AI companies about how foreign actors are trying to extract data.
  • Help private companies coordinate better to defend against such attacks.
  • Work with industry to create best practices to detect and stop these activities.
  • Build stronger systems to prevent large-scale AI data extraction.
  • Explore actions to hold foreign players accountable for these campaigns.

data.title: Stocks still have a lot of upside despite current downturn – Deutsche Bank’s Chadha
data.description:
Bankim Chadha, Deutsche Bank’s chief U.S. equity and global strategist, sees significant room for stocks to climb, projecting a potential 9% to 10% upside for the S&P 500 (SP500). The bullish outlook stems from a substantial gap between current market positioning and strong earnings growth.

Chadha, in an interview with CNBC, noted that he is not surprised by the market’s resilience amid geopolitical risks, higher oil prices (CL1:COM), (CO1:COM), and inflation concerns.

He explained that the market has followed a typical historical pattern, with a sharp sell-off followed by rapid recovery.

“The average or typical behavior is a sharp sell-off three weeks down, 6%-8%,” Chadha said. “And I think the surprising thing for most people is the market tends to recover completely in another three weeks.”

The strategist pointed to a significant shift in market positioning as a key factor in the recent rally. He observed that positioning jumped from underweight to neutral in one of the biggest one-week increases on record, though he emphasized that the focus should be on the fundamental drivers behind these moves rather than the positioning labels themselves.

A notable disconnect exists between what the market is currently pricing in and actual earnings performance. While neutral positioning reflects expectations of 8% earnings growth, blended estimates from recent reports show 21% growth.

“If you mechanically took positioning today to where it should be with earnings, that’s 9% or 10% up in the S&P 500 (SP500) from where we are,” Chadha explained.

The strategist also addressed the recent sector rotations, noting that the shift out of mega-cap growth (VUG), (IWF), (IVW), tech (VGT), (XLK), (IYW), and financials (XLF), (VFH), (IYF) into the broader market is now largely complete.

He pointed out that financials positioning had fallen to the second percentile, comparable to levels seen during past financial crises, before rebounding. These two sectors led the recovery because they had the largest gap between earnings growth and positioning.

Looking ahead, Chadha suggested that once shorter-term geopolitical events subside, the market will return to its underlying trends. Given the historical patterns and current positioning dynamics, the market’s resilience should not come as a surprise to investors.

data.title: ConnectOne targets 3.50% year-end spot net interest margin while maintaining mid-single-digit loan growth outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ConnectOne Bancorp, Inc. (CNOB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We kick off 2026 with strong momentum, firing on all cylinders as demonstrated by our results" (Chairman, President & CEO Frank Sorrentino), adding that the company "scaled the balance sheet from under $10 billion to nearly $15 billion in assets" and said its footprint "now spans the entire New York City metro region and naturally extends to the growing South Florida market."

  • "Reflecting our success and confidence in future performance, we opportunistically repurchased shares in the first quarter and increased our common dividend" (Chairman, President & CEO Sorrentino), while also emphasizing expense discipline: "we remain highly disciplined as we continue to realize merger synergies" and said this is being "further enhanced" by "the thoughtful integration of AI across the organization."

  • "For the first quarter, we reported operating earnings per share of $0.79" (Senior EVP & CFO William Burns) and said results were driven by margin and growth: "our net interest margin... expanded by 12 basis points sequentially to 3.39%" and "loan originations were strong... approximately 10%" annualized, including "$300 million in growth for the quarter."

  • "Delinquencies increased due to an isolated client relationship collateralized by 19 multifamily New York City rent-stabilized properties" (Chairman, President & CEO Sorrentino), while also stating, "we've always been supporters of affordable housing" and that the company has "a strong track record of successfully resolving situations."

Outlook

  • "In terms of margin outlook, we are maintaining our previous guidance. It's a year-end spot margin of 3.50%" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns), adding that the outlook assumes "lower probability of rate cuts, maybe there's one to come," along with "loans repricing higher" amid "a competitive deposit pricing environment."

  • "The pipeline remains strong and portfolio growth net of payoffs is anticipated to be in the mid-single digits" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns).

  • "I'm targeting a 1.5% per quarter sequential growth rate going forward" for noninterest expenses (Senior EVP & CFO Burns).

  • "Our plan is to do about 100,000 a quarter for the next -- for the rest of the year" in share repurchases (Senior EVP & CFO Burns), while adding, "it could depend on what the stock price is as well as what our growth rates are."

Financial Results

  • "Operating PPNR as a percentage of average assets" was "1.81%" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns), and he highlighted margin momentum: "this current quarter exceeded our initial projections" and said it was driven by "contractual loan repricings and improved deposit costs."

  • "Our total nonperforming assets declined to just 0.29% of total assets" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns), and he said "criticized and classified loans dropped" to "2.26% of total loans" while "net charge-offs" on the non-PCG portfolio were "just 8 basis points annualized."

  • On the rent-stabilized portfolio, "our total rent stabilized portfolio has been reduced over the past year to $675 million" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns). He said the acquired component included "reserves and yield adjustments aggregating to $66 million" and the originated portion had an "elevated reserve" of "$15 million," concluding: "we have a 12% offset... providing more than $80 million in total value absorbing cushion."

  • "The provision for loan losses for the first quarter was $5.2 million" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns). He also reported "noninterest expenses were $55.7 million" and "noninterest income was $6.8 million" for the quarter.

  • "Tangible book value per share increased by 1.7% to $23.93" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns), and he said the board declared "an 8.3% increase in our common dividend" while the company "repurchased 90,000 shares in the quarter at $26.21 per share" and had "more than 500,000 shares remaining" in authorization.

Q&A

  • Tyler Cacciator, Stephens Inc., Research Division: asked about drivers of quarterly loan growth and whether growth could land above the mid-single-digit view; Chairman, President & CEO Sorrentino said, "payoffs have come down a little bit" and added, "the mid-single digits is probably where we feel the most comfortable. It could be a little higher, it could be a little lower."

  • Tyler Cacciator, Stephens Inc., Research Division: asked about new origination spreads and pipeline; Senior EVP & CFO Burns said, "The pipeline right now was about $635 million" and added, "I think the spreads are being maintained nicely."

  • Tyler Cacciator, Stephens Inc., Research Division: asked whether past-due rent-regulated loans were legacy vs. acquired and about broader portfolio impacts; Senior EVP & CFO Burns replied, "First one is from the legacy portfolio," and Chairman, President & CEO Sorrentino said, "there are challenges in the rent-stabilized portfolio" and added, "I'm fairly optimistic" given portfolio positioning and reserves.

  • Timothy DeLacey, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: asked about Florida and Orlando expansion; Chairman, President & CEO Sorrentino said, "we're very bullish on the Florida market," noting headcount growth "past 18 or 19 individuals" and said activity remains "a great mix of both C&I" and real estate lending.

  • Timothy DeLacey, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: asked about deposit costs and purchase accounting accretion; Senior EVP & CFO Burns said deposit costs are planned "to be flat for the year" and later provided: "it was $9.3 million in the most recent quarter... and for '27, it would be $8 million a quarter."

  • Emily Lee, KBW: asked about payout ratio/capital deployment and later LTVs on rent-regulated loans; Senior EVP & CFO Burns said, "We've always liked to have a lower payout ratio" and on LTVs added, "We don't -- nothing at this time" due to market flux.

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: asked about M&A backdrop; Chairman, President & CEO Sorrentino said, "We're highly focused on our organic growth" and concluded, "I don't see anything right at the moment that's compelling."

  • Emily Lee, KBW: asked about AI use cases; Chairman, President & CEO Sorrentino said, "AI is pervasive" and described use of "nCino and Slack and Google... which has Gemini built into it," adding the goal is to "scale faster and better with less human resources" while improving "accuracy."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was neutral-to-skeptical, concentrating on loan growth durability, deposit pricing pressure, rent-stabilized risk, and capital allocation, including questions on whether the past-due uptick could signal broader exposure and requests for LTV metrics.

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks and generally steady in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing momentum and capital actions, while acknowledging uncertainty on rent-stabilized valuations ("it's difficult to determine exactly what the current LTVs... are" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns)) and framing reserves as a buffer ("strong reserves... give us some comfort" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns)).

  • Versus last quarter’s framing that margins would rise with rate cuts, management in Q1 leaned more on loan repricing and maintaining guidance "even without the benefit of further rate cuts" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns), reflecting a more rate-cut-agnostic emphasis.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language on margin shifted from Q4’s step-by-step sensitivity to cuts and other offsets to Q1’s reaffirmation: "we are maintaining our previous guidance... a year-end spot margin of 3.50%" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns).

  • Loan growth commentary moved from Q4’s expectation of "3% to 5%" loan growth (Senior EVP & CFO Burns, Q4) to Q1’s view that growth "is anticipated to be in the mid-single digits" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns, Q1), supported by Q1’s "$300 million" quarterly increase.

  • Q4 highlighted branch consolidation plans ("we've identified 5 branch locations to consolidate" (Chairman, President & CEO Sorrentino, Q4)), while Q1’s prepared remarks put more emphasis on "optimizing all systems" and AI integration as a driver of efficiency (Chairman, President & CEO Sorrentino, Q1).

  • Analyst focus tightened in Q1 around rent-stabilized multifamily specifics after management disclosed an isolated delinquency tied to "19" properties (Chairman, President & CEO Sorrentino, Q1); Q4 discussion of credit issues was more general, citing a multifamily relationship affecting NPAs.

Risks and Concerns

  • "30 to 59 day delinquencies... rose to 0.81%" due to "one relationship" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns), with management highlighting mitigation through portfolio marks/reserves and active workout engagement.

  • Deposit pricing remained a watch item as management cited "a competitive deposit pricing environment" (Senior EVP & CFO Burns) and indicated loan growth was partly funded by "some wholesale deposits" alongside reductions in cash and securities.

  • On rent-stabilized exposure uncertainty, management emphasized the market is "in state of flux" and that LTVs are hard to determine (Senior EVP & CFO Burns), while pointing to an aggregate reserve/mark cushion and portfolio reduction to $675 million.

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 as a momentum quarter driven by a 3.39% net interest margin and $300 million of loan growth, while keeping its year-end spot margin target at 3.50% and expecting mid-single-digit loan growth net of payoffs. Alongside an 8.3% dividend increase and continued buybacks, management addressed investor sensitivity around New York City rent-stabilized multifamily by disclosing a single troubled relationship tied to 19 properties and pointing to portfolio reductions and more than $80 million of aggregate marks and reserves as its principal cushion.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Micron tops chip stocks Quant ranking as SOX crosses 10,000 for the first time
data.description:
The PHLX Semiconductor Index (SOX) surged past the 10,000 mark for the first time on record, marking a symbolic milestone for a sector that has become the backbone of the global equity rally.

In light of this, below is a list of the top 10 semiconductor and semiconductor materials & equipment stocks ranked according to their Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings. The list was generated using Seeking Alpha’s quantitative screening process to identify the highest-rated stocks in these sectors.

Micron Technology (MU) tops the list with a near-perfect Quant Rating of 4.99. Ichor Holdings (ICHR) and AXT, Inc. (AXTI) follow closely behind, each with ratings of 4.98, while Ultra Clean Holdings (UCTT) rounds out the top tier with a 4.97 rating. Major industry players Intel (INTC) and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) also appear within the top 10, with ratings of 4.87 and 4.85, respectively.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant system ranks stocks based on their relative performance on critical quantitative measures, including valuation, growth, stock momentum, and profitability. Each stock is rated on a scale of 1 to 5, with any rating of 3.5 or above considered a bullish rating (Buy or Strong Buy), while a score of 2.5 or below represents a bearish rating (Sell or Strong Sell).

Here is the list:

  1. Micron Technology, Inc. (MU), Quant Rating: 4.99

  2. Ichor Holdings, Ltd. (ICHR), Quant Rating: 4.98

  3. AXT, Inc. (AXTI), Quant Rating: 4.98

  4. Ultra Clean Holdings, Inc. (UCTT), Quant Rating: 4.97

  5. MaxLinear, Inc. (MXL), Quant Rating: 4.95

  6. Navitas Semiconductor Corporation (NVTS), Quant Rating: 4.93

  7. Onto Innovation Inc. (ONTO), Quant Rating: 4.93

  8. Intel Corporation (INTC), Quant Rating: 4.87

  9. Diodes Incorporated (DIOD), Quant Rating: 4.85

  10. Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD), Quant Rating: 4.85

Semiconductor ETFs: (SMH), (SOXX), (SOXL), (FTXL), (XSD), (USD), (PSI), and (SEMI)

data.title: Chevron resumes full production at Wheatstone LNG after cyclone damage shutdown
data.description:
Chevron (CVX) said Thursday it resumed full production of liquefied natural gas at its Wheatstone plant in Australia following repairs ​for damage caused by a ⁠cyclone last month.

Tropical Cyclone Narelle disrupted operations at the 8.9M metric tons/year facility last month, leaving both LNG processing trains offline, but Chevron (CVX) said it was able to ​restart gas production ​for ⁠Western Australian customers in "around a week" after the cyclone, ⁠with ​LNG returning progressively.

The cyclone also hit Chevron's (CVX) Gorgon LNG facility, which had to suspend operations at just one of its three liquefaction trains.

The disruptions came as LNG supply from Qatar dried up following Iran's closure of the Strait of Hormuz and strikes on Qatari LNG infrastructure.

The 30-day moving average of net shipments of LNG to Asia plunged below 600K tons this past weekend, the lowest one-month moving average of LNG arrivals into Asia since June 2020, according to ship-tracking data compiled by Bloomberg.

data.title: Huntington targets 18%-19% ROTCE in 2027 and reiterates $1.90-$1.93 EPS path while lifting fee growth outlook to 31%-33%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Huntington Bancshares (HBAN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “We delivered an outstanding first quarter by all measures, driven by disciplined execution across the franchise that is translating into strong profitability and returns,” said (Chairman, President & CEO Stephen Steinour), while adding that conditions in Huntington’s footprint are “remaining consistent with prior quarters” and “these factors do not change our outlook for performance this year.”

  • Steinour pointed to integration and expansion priorities, citing “our Carolinas expansion,” “the build-out of our vertical specialty businesses,” “partnerships with Cadence and Veritex,” and “the Janney and TM Capital acquisitions,” and said Huntington “made the decision to temporarily build additional liquidity, improving our already peer-leading liquidity position.”

  • Steinour raised profitability targets, saying, “we raised our ROTCE target to 18% to 19%,” and added, “We remain confident in our ability to deliver that level of profitability in 2027.”

  • “For the quarter, earnings per common share was $0.25,” said (CFO & Senior EVP Zachary Wasserman), adding, “On an adjusted basis… EPS was $0.37,” alongside “Net interest income increased $301 million or 18.7% sequentially” and “Net interest margin was 3.24%.”

  • Wasserman highlighted capital return actions and capacity: “As noted at a conference in March, we increased our 2026 share repurchase plans to $550 million,” and “the Board approved a new $3 billion share repurchase authorization, replacing the prior program.”

Outlook

  • Management reiterated its medium-term targets, with Wasserman stating, “The second key milestone is our 2027 earnings per share projection of $1.90 to $1.93 with a return on tangible common equity of between 18% and 19%.”

  • Wasserman updated key 2026 guideposts tied to mix and liquidity decisions: “we now expect 2026 NIM to trend into the high 3.20s compared to our prior expectation in the mid-3.30s,” and said Huntington elected “to carry approximately $4 billion of incremental Fed cash,” which “does reduce reported NIM.”

  • Fees were the major upside lever in the 2026 outlook. Wasserman said, “we're raising our expectations for fee revenue growth by 4 percentage points to 31% to 33%,” while also stating, “we're tightening our 2026 expense growth range to the lower half of the 32.5% to 33.5% range.”

  • Compared with Q4 2025 commentary, the tone shifted from initial stand-alone plus Cadence framing to optimization and mix management. In Q4, Wasserman guided “fee revenues to grow between 13% and 16%,” whereas in Q1 he described raising fee growth expectations to “31% to 33%” and said NII should be “at the low end of our guided range.”

Financial Results

  • EPS was $0.25 vs. $0.35 (analysts’ estimate for Q1 2026). Adjusted EPS was $0.37 vs. $0.35 (analysts’ estimate for Q1 2026).

  • Wasserman described balance sheet growth excluding Cadence: “loan balances increased 1.5% or $2.2 billion,” and “core deposits increased $3.8 billion or 2.3% quarter-over-quarter,” while emphasizing disciplined pricing.

  • Net interest income and margin expanded, with management attributing margin drivers to “lower funding costs, reduced hedge drag and purchase accounting,” while noting the liquidity move: “we elected to add approximately $4 billion of higher cash balances at the Fed to further strengthen our liquidity profile.”

  • Fee performance was framed as a standout, including “Payments revenue increased 21% year-over-year,” “Wealth Management revenue grew 19%,” and “Capital Markets delivered its strongest revenue quarter on record,” with Wasserman adding that capital markets revenue (excluding acquisitions) “grew nearly 60% year-over-year.”

Q&A

  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC: asked about “the balance sheet optimization project” and how success is measured; (CFO Wasserman) said the bank is calibrating to “a little softer” growth outlook, aims to tune down “commercial real estate… construction,” and expects deposits to grow “at or above the growth of loans,” calling the result “a profit-neutral outcome for this year.”

  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC: asked why a “$3 billion buyback authorization” versus a $550 million plan; (CFO Wasserman) said the industry is moving toward “an evergreen authorization,” reiterated “approximately $550 million of share repurchases this year,” and added, “Basel III represents an opportunity.”

  • Erika Najarian, UBS: pressed on incremental cost actions and Cadence retention concerns; (CFO Wasserman) said baseline tuning was “about $50 million,” while (President, consumer & regional banking Brantley Standridge) said “overall retention remains very, very strong,” and Steinour added credit philosophy differences can create “a little bit of a friction for some colleagues.”

  • Erika Najarian, UBS: asked for Basel III Endgame detail and what the market may be missing; (CFO Wasserman) said the standardized approach implies “about 7.7%… 7.5% to 8% reduction in RWA,” representing “approximately 80 basis points of reported CET1 benefit,” and said, “we… are very, very frustrated that the fundamental… performance… is not being represented in the valuation of the company.”

  • John Pancari, Evercore: asked about Fed cash decision and loan spreads; (CFO Wasserman) cited “modest spread compression… between 5 and 15 basis points,” and (CEO Steinour) said, “the Middle East issues are what drove us to that decision.”

  • John Pancari, Evercore: asked about incremental whole-bank M&A; (CEO Steinour) said, “The primary focus for us is driving organic growth,” and added, “I don't see us doing anything big… nothing imminent.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to skeptical, with repeated clarification requests on guidance, capital, Basel III, and integration; UBS’s Najarian said, “I'm scratching my head a little bit,” and asked, “What do you all think the market is not fully understanding about your story?”

  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and more defensive/firm in Q&A around valuation and execution; Wasserman said, “we… are very, very frustrated,” while emphasizing controllables and concluding that by Q4 “that will be so manifestly obvious that the valuation… can only recover from there.”

  • Versus Q4 2025, the Q1 exchange showed more direct probing on market underperformance and Basel III mechanics, while management in both quarters reiterated the same medium-term framework (2027 targets, synergy delivery) but in Q1 leaned more on “optimization,” liquidity actions, and expense contingency levers.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Profitability targets moved higher in Q1 as Steinour said Huntington “raised our ROTCE target to 18% to 19%,” while Q4 emphasized delivering within a “flywheel” model and initiating repurchases “following the close” of Cadence.

  • Guidance language shifted from Q4’s stand-alone framing (including Cadence contribution ranges) to Q1’s calibration and mix actions, including NIM now expected in “the high 3.20s” (vs. prior “mid-3.30s”), paired with a higher fee growth outlook.

  • Analyst focus broadened from Q4’s heavy modeling questions on expenses and integration math to Q1’s combination of guidance sensitivity, Basel III capital effects, share authorization size, and talent retention.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macro and geopolitical uncertainty, with Steinour saying Huntington is operating in “a dynamic global environment with geopolitical developments adding complexity,” and specifically pointing to “the Middle East issues” as driving incremental Fed cash liquidity.

  • Credit and portfolio risks centered on CRE construction. Wasserman said Huntington will “tune lower… commercial real estate, in particular, construction,” and (Chief credit officer Brendan Lawlor) said it is an area the bank will “reduce our exposure to… in an organic fashion over the next 2-plus years.”

  • Competition for deposits remained a watch item. Standridge said the environment is “competitive” but “rational,” and described multiple growth levers including Carolinas branch builds and new-market digital launches.

Final Takeaway

Huntington’s management framed Q1 2026 as a quarter of strong profitability and record fee momentum while it integrates Cadence and Veritex, raises its 2027 ROTCE target to 18% to 19%, and reiterates a 2027 EPS path of $1.90 to $1.93. The company tempered net interest margin expectations to the high 3.20s (from mid-3.30s) tied to balance sheet optimization and carrying $4 billion of incremental Fed cash for liquidity, while lifting its 2026 fee revenue growth outlook to 31% to 33% and tightening expense growth toward the low end of its range. In Q&A, analysts focused on guidance mechanics, capital return capacity (including a new $3 billion authorization), Basel III RWA relief (management cited a 7.5% to 8% reduction), and talent retention and credit philosophy alignment in acquired footprints.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Salesforce tumbles, ending six-day gains as AI fears weigh
data.description:
Shares of Salesforce(CRM) slipped about 9% in the afternoon trading on Thursday, snapping a six-session winning streak, as renewed concerns over the long-term impact of artificial intelligence on software companies weighed on investor sentiment.

The stock has gained around 6.87% between April 15 and April 22, compared to a 1.64% rise in the broader index.

Shares came under pressure as investor concerns around the long-term impact of artificial intelligence on the software sector weighed on sentiment, even as near-term growth remains solid.

Analysts have flagged that while Salesforce is expected to post its fastest revenue growth in years, that may not be sufficient to offset fears that AI tools could disrupt core software business models, compress margins, and intensify competition, raising broader questions about the durability of growth across the industry.

According to Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating system, CRM is rated a Hold, with a score of 3.15 out of 5, receiving an A+ for profitability, but a D+ both in terms of growth and momentum.

Meanwhile, a recent Seeking Alpha analysis said Salesforce continues to show improving fundamentals despite a sharp pullback in its stock, adding that AI-driven offerings are beginning to support future growth expectations, while the company’s balance sheet remains underleveraged. He pointed out that, “AI-driven products like Agentforce show strong adoption, supporting future growth reacceleration into the low-to-mid teens.”

While on Wall Street, 35 out of 46 analysts rate the stock a buy or higher, 10 recommend a hold, and one of them suggests a sell or lower.

Shares have fallen around 10.4% in the past month and have declined approximately 34.5% year-to-date.

data.title: Waste Connections anticipates $100M of M&A revenue closings by end of Q2 or early Q3 while reaffirming $1.4B-$1.45B 2026 free cash flow
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Waste Connections (WCN) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Ronald Mittelstaedt (Founder, CEO, President & Director) said the company “exceeded expectations for revenue and EBITDA” and delivered “EBITDA margin of 32.5%, up 90 basis points year-over-year, excluding commodity impacts,” despite “outsized weather impacts” and higher fuel.

  • CEO Mittelstaedt tied Q1 pricing momentum to full-year visibility: “core price of 6%, providing visibility for the high end of our full year 2026 outlook of 5% to 5.5%,” and pointed to “higher special waste tons, up 8% year-over-year in Q1.”

  • CEO Mittelstaedt framed fuel as timing-driven, citing U.S. diesel up “12% year-over-year” and said Q1 fuel costs ran “about $5 million above our expectations,” while emphasizing hedges “for over 45% of our expected diesel requirements for 2026” and surcharge recovery stepping up in Q2.

  • Mary Whitney (Executive VP & CFO) provided the quarter’s topline and profitability: “revenue of $2.371 billion exceeded our expectations,” and “adjusted EBITDA… was $769.5 million, up 8% year-over-year.”

  • CEO Mittelstaedt highlighted capital allocation and M&A visibility: a “handful of deals with aggregate annualized revenue of approximately $100 million expected to close by the end of Q2 or early Q3,” plus “year-to-date outlays of approximately 365 million” for buybacks, repurchasing “about 1% of shares outstanding.”

  • On Chiquita Canyon, CEO Mittelstaedt said the ETLF event is “stable, controlled and decelerating,” adding: “There is no change in our 2026 outlook for Chiquita, which reflects free cash flow impacts of $100 million to $150 million.”

Outlook

  • CEO Mittelstaedt said, “we haven't seen anything to date that doesn't support our full year outlook as provided in February,” while also stating “upside potential from recent trends,” including commodities and “ongoing investments in human capital and AI.”

  • CFO Whitney reiterated pricing visibility: “With over 75% of our price increases already in place or contractually provided for, we have high visibility for full year 2026 core pricing at the high end of the range we provided or about 5.5%.”

  • On fuel, CFO Whitney described quarter-by-quarter timing pressure: “Q2 would be the toughest,” adding, “By Q3, you're more at that 100% level,” in reference to surcharge recovery timing.

  • Compared with Q4 2025 guidance language, management again emphasized a conservative stance in Q1 2026, with CEO Mittelstaedt stating the current macro “doesn't support” changing the February outlook and describing potential commodity and E&P benefits as additive rather than embedded.

Financial Results

  • CFO Whitney said revenue increased “$143 million or 6.4% year-over-year,” including “$55 million” from acquisitions net of divestitures; organic growth in solid waste collection/transfer/disposal was “3.1%.”

  • CFO Whitney quantified volumes and pricing mechanics: “Organic growth… was led by 6% core price,” while “yield of 4.7%… implies solid waste volumes down about 1.5%,” including “up to about 0.5 point attributable to outsized weather events.”

  • CFO Whitney detailed landfill mix: “Total tons were up 4% on MSW up 5% and special waste up 8%… partially offset by… C&D down 5%,” and said recycled commodity revenue improved, citing OCC at “$89 per ton in Q1” and exiting the quarter “in line with the 2025 full year average price of $94 per ton.”

  • CFO Whitney said adjusted free cash flow was “$246 million,” and reaffirmed full-year adjusted free cash flow of “$1.4 billion to $1.45 billion.” She also noted the company issued “a $600 million note offering,” ending with “debt outstanding of about $9.1 billion,” “net debt-to-EBITDA… about 2.75x,” and “liquidity of approximately $1 billion.”

Q&A

  • Patrick Brown, Raymond James: Asked whether fuel is an EBITDA “push,” margin dilution from surcharges, and Q2 fuel lag; Mary Whitney (Executive VP & CFO): “Q2 would be the toughest,” and discussed hedges and lagged recovery, illustrating potential “$60 million or $70 million in incremental fuel surcharges” if fuel stays elevated.

  • Konark Gupta, Scotiabank: Pressed on margin upside vs. prior “50 to 70” bps commentary and asked about M&A mix; Mary Whitney (Executive VP & CFO): said Q1 strength “could be arguably another indication… tailwind” but cautioned benefits may not repeat at the same pace; Ronald Mittelstaedt (Founder, CEO, President & Director): clarified it is “a series of transactions” consistent with “singles and doubles.”

  • Toni Kaplan, Morgan Stanley: Asked if full-year volume remains “flat to down 50” and what’s needed; Mary Whitney (Executive VP & CFO): said construction-driven activity “we haven't seen accelerate yet,” while “reduction in the shedding” is helping; Ronald Mittelstaedt (Founder, CEO, President & Director): linked retention improvements to AI pricing, “up to almost a 20% improvement in retention/churn.”

  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs: Asked about E&P revenue framing and longer-term margin expansion; Mary Whitney (Executive VP & CFO): said organic expectations remain limited until drilling picks up; Ronald Mittelstaedt (Founder, CEO, President & Director): targeted “somewhere approaching about 100 basis points of margin appreciation as we head into '28” from seven AI initiatives.

  • Trevor Romeo, William Blair: Asked about mothballed E&P facilities and New York City waste zones timing; Ronald Mittelstaedt (Founder, CEO, President & Director): said 2 of 5 mothballed sites reopened and remaining could add “$2 million to $5 million in EBITDA per facility,” and NYC zones are seeing a “6- to 12-month delay” with full implementation now “the middle of '28 to the end of '28.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral-to-slightly negative on near-term mechanics (fuel lag, margins, volume) with repeated precision-seeking, including “Can you maybe size some of the dilution” (Patrick Brown, Raymond James) and multiple E&P follow-ups.

  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks (“upside potential,” “high visibility”) and more measured in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing timing and uncertainty around drilling (“too early” on producer reaction) and cautioning against extrapolation.

  • Versus Q4 2025, Q1 2026 sentiment shifted toward fuel and near-term cadence questions, while management confidence remained steady around pricing, M&A pipeline, and longer-run AI benefits.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 reiterated Q4 2025’s full-year 2026 pricing framework (5% to 5.5%) but raised confidence to “the high end” via Q1 “core price of 6%” and stronger retention.

  • Q1 placed more emphasis on fuel volatility and lagged recovery than Q4, with explicit callouts to a March diesel spike and Q2 being “the toughest,” while Q4 focused more heavily on laying out the annual guidance ranges.

  • Q1 provided more concrete AI ROI framing, with CEO Mittelstaedt stating the seven initiatives could drive “about 100 basis points” of margin appreciation into 2028, extending beyond Q4’s discussion of AI rollout and benefits.

Risks and Concerns

  • CEO Mittelstaedt cited “a volatile macroeconomic and geopolitical backdrop,” and said a sustained crude-driven shift could be additive but depends on time and rig mobilization.

  • Management flagged fuel timing risk (lagged recovery) and margin optics from surcharges, with CFO Whitney noting Q2 is expected to carry the most recovery lag.

  • On Chiquita Canyon, CEO Mittelstaedt maintained the $100 million to $150 million 2026 free cash flow impact, while stating an accrual adjustment was made in Q1 and that a longer-term EPA agreement is being pursued for “greater clarity.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a start that supports the February full-year framework, anchored by core price strength, improving special waste, and ongoing margin execution despite weather and fuel cost spikes. Leadership reiterated confidence in high-end 2026 pricing visibility and a disciplined capital plan combining an active acquisition pipeline (about $100 million of annualized revenue expected to close by end of Q2 or early Q3) with share repurchases, while maintaining the Chiquita Canyon free cash flow impact outlook and positioning AI initiatives as a multiyear margin driver toward 2028.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Lockheed Martin says Peru will buy 12 F-16 jets amid political dispute over deal
data.description:
Lockheed Martin (LMT) on Thursday said Peru’s government announced plans to purchase 12 F-16 Block 70 fighter aircraft, a move the U.S. defense contractor said would help modernize the South American nation’s air force and upgrade its aging fighter fleet.

The company said the aircraft would be built at its Greenville, South Carolina, facility. Financial terms weren't disclosed in the statement.

 

 

The announcement comes against the backdrop of political turmoil in Lima over a separate and larger proposed fighter-jet acquisition. Earlier this week, Peru’s defense and foreign affairs ministers resigned after accusing interim President Jose Maria Balcázar of misleading the public about a previously planned $3.5 billion deal for 24 F-16 jets.

According to statements from the departing officials, contracts tied to that earlier purchase were signed by the military despite Balcázar publicly saying the matter should be left to Peru’s next elected government.

Balcázar denied deceiving the public and said he had not intervened in the negotiations. In a televised address, he said national defense needs must be weighed against other priorities facing the country.

Political transition clouds procurement path

Peru is being led by an interim administration that is scheduled to leave office in July after a presidential runoff election in June. The uncertainty has complicated major policy decisions, including defense spending.

The president and finance ministry retain authority over public expenditures tied to any military purchase, making funding approvals a key issue even if procurement contracts are signed.

Several presidential contenders have backed proceeding with a fighter acquisition, while others have questioned whether billions of dollars should be directed toward defense instead of social programs.

Long-running fighter replacement effort

Peru has been evaluating options to replace older combat aircraft for several years. Military officials have argued that renewing the fleet is necessary to maintain operational readiness and national airspace control.

Lockheed Martin (LMT) said Peru would join 29 other nations operating the F-16 if the purchase proceeds. The company described the Block 70 variant as the latest production version of the aircraft, featuring updated radar and avionics systems.

The F-16 remains one of the most widely used fighter platforms globally, with operators spanning Europe, Asia, the Middle East and Latin America.
